metadata.title: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. TEAL = phase hand / rotating; CRIMSON = probability density / shadow / still
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: German. Medhavy 1-word budget. Wagwan is Bear-only.
metadata.audio_note: All existing inner-beat mp3s were generated with ElevenLabs NBB voice. Must regenerate ALL with kokoro af_kore for Medhavy: python3 runtime/scripts/generate_audio.py quantum-mechanics-vol1/youtube/vox-spinning-phase

beats.narration_text: The clock-and-shadow image landed: the wave function spins as e to the minus i E t over h-bar while the squared length holds still, so stationary means the shadow, not the clock. I want to make the shadow move. Please: (1) confirm the roughly 1.5 times ten to the fifteen turns per second for a 1 electron-volt state, (2) superpose two eigenstates with different energies and derive the beat frequency at which the probability density actually oscillates, and (3) show why a single eigenstate can never produce that motion.

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: The clock-and-shadow image landed: the wave function spins as e to the minus i E t over h-bar while the squared length holds still, so stationary means the shadow, not the clock. I want to make the shadow move. Please: (1) confirm the roughly 1.5 times ten to the fifteen turns per second for a 1 electron-volt state, (2) superpose two eigenstates with different energies and derive the beat frequency at which the probability density actually oscillates, and (3) show why a single eigenstate can never produce that motion.

beats.narration_text: A stationary state is called stationary because nothing you can measure about it changes — yet the wave function underneath is spinning the whole time.

card.copy: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a repeated measurement histogram for a ground-state electron — show the same position distribution appearing each time, labeled 'stationary: no change with time'"

beats.narration_text: Measure an electron's position in its ground state — same histogram every time, no matter when you do it. Stationary means: no observable thing changes with time.

Yet the Schrodinger equation demands the wave function change. The time factor is e to the minus i E t over h-bar. That's not constant — it rotates in the complex plane. Why doesn't this change anything?

card.copy: The wave function has a rotating phase. Measurements stay constant. How can a spinning thing cast a still shadow?

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a complex number represented as a clock hand in the complex plane, with a length and angle, rotating continuously — label length and phase angle"

beats.narration_text: The wave function is a complex number at each point. Think of it as a clock hand — it has a length and a direction in the complex plane.

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a clock hand spinning at rate E/hbar in the complex plane — show two energy levels spinning at different rates side by side, higher energy spins faster"

beats.narration_text: In a stationary state, that clock hand spins at a rate set by the energy — E divided by h-bar. Faster energy level, faster rotation.

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a spinning clock hand casting a shadow on the floor — the shadow stays still as the hand rotates because |psi|^2 is the squared length, not the angle"

beats.narration_text: Now the probability density: it's the length of the clock hand squared. The length doesn't change as it spins. The shadow on the floor holds still.

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a clock (wave function) spinning on the left, its shadow (probability density) motionless on the right — label 'wave function spins' and 'shadow holds still'"

beats.narration_text: The clock is the wave function. The shadow is |psi| squared. The wave function spins; the probability density doesn't. Stationary means the shadow, not the clock.

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two energy eigenstates with clocks spinning at different rates omega1 and omega2 — combine them and show the probability density starting to oscillate as the angle between the clocks changes"

beats.narration_text: This is why energy eigenstates are special: their probabilities are time-independent even though the wave functions aren't. Mix two eigenstates and the rotation rates differ — then the shadow starts to move.

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a ground state at 1 eV with its phase rotating at 1.5e15 turns per second — show the rotation rate vs visible light frequency for scale comparison"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: a ground state with energy 1 eV. Its phase rotates at about 1.5 times 10 to the 15 turns per second — a million times faster than visible light oscillates. The spin is real, just undetectable by itself.
The wave function is a spinning clock. The probability density is its shadow on the floor. The shadow holds still. The clock never stops.

card.copy: The wave function spins. The probability is its shadow. The shadow holds still.

beats.narration_text: The phase is spinning right now, in every stationary state. Paste this in and watch it.

props.segment: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
props.command: claude "animate the phase of the ground state of a particle in a box as a spinning phasor — show the complex plane and the probability density side by side"

beats.narration_text: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
